name: Quicksort

x.io: Input: a mutable random-access sequence and a comparator. Output: the same sequence permuted into sorted order, in place.
preconditions: Elements admit a strict weak ordering
Random-access (O(1)) indexing for pivot selection and partition
correctness: Partition invariant: everything left of the pivot ≤ pivot ≤ everything right, at every level
Terminates: each recursive call operates on a strictly smaller range
x.stability: Not stable — equal elements may be reordered. Determinism depends on pivot choice; randomised/median pivots make the worst case input-independent.
x.parallelisable: Yes — the two partitions are independent and recurse in parallel (fork/join); the partition step itself is mostly sequential.
dataStructures: Often an explicit stack to bound depth (introsort)

def: An in-place comparison sort that partitions around a pivot and recurses on each side.
problem: Sort a sequence in-place with excellent average performance and low constants.
mechanism: Choose a pivot, partition elements ≤/≥ pivot, recurse. Real implementations (introsort) switch to heapsort on bad recursion depth and insertion sort for small runs.
benefits: O(n log n) average with tiny constants
limits: O(n²) worst case with naive pivots
myths: "Always O(n log n)" — only average; worst case is quadratic
sectors: All sectors via std lib sorts
